storm.summary_layperson: Rain showers are within 10 miles to the west and heading toward the cove, with two more clusters around 27 miles out — expect passing showers this evening, but very low instability makes a strong thunderstorm unlikely.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The Sound water (67°F) is nearly the same temperature as the air today, so there's no cool marine layer to deflect or weaken incoming storms — the cove will see similar conditions to nearby inland areas.
storm.detail_html:
Radar shows three storm clusters approaching from the west and WSW. The nearest cell is 9.8 miles to the west at 36 dBZ, with two more clusters at 27–28 miles bearing WSW at 36–37 dBZ. The NWS forecast gives a 50% chance of rain showers this afternoon, with south winds around 6 mph.

Instability is very low: HRRR puts CAPE at 50 J/kg, and the NWS Area Forecast Discussion confirms CAPE remains under 500 J/kg — described as "tall and skinny." With this little available energy, storm updrafts can't sustain themselves long enough to organize into a severe threat. Bulk shear aloft is a notable 37 kt (WSW), which in principle favors organized storm structure, but without the instability to tap it that shear has little effect at the surface.

The Long Island Sound's protective marine-layer effect is absent today. The Sound water is 67°F against an air temperature of 70°F — only a 2.3°F differential. The stable coastal boundary layer that sometimes deflects or weakens storms before they reach the cove requires at least an 8–10°F temperature gap; today's conditions don't support it. The cove should expect the same weather as towns 2–5 miles inland.

Most likely scenario: the nearest cluster produces a passing rain shower reaching the cove within the next 30–60 minutes. The two additional clusters 27–28 miles out may generate follow-on showers through the evening. Brief gusty outflows from these cells (10–20 kt) are possible but are unlikely to be severe given the low-CAPE environment.

What would change this call: a significant rise in CAPE — for instance, from post-shower surface heating if skies partially clear between clusters — could allow one cell to produce more intense rainfall or a brief thunderstorm. Monitor radar for any cell strengthening above 45 dBZ.

hourly.shift_note: light southerly — best of the evening

wind.tactics_blurb: Conditions are too light and variable for reliable tactical reads — expect 0–4 kt in random directions all evening. If racing, stay near the center; any shift favoring a side is likely to reverse before you can commit to it.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime: B-light. The thermal driver is absent today — the inland-to-Sound temperature differential is only 2.3°F (KHPN 70°F vs BRHC3 67°F Sound water), well below the 5°F gate required for a thermally driven sea breeze. Without that driver, any wind for the evening comes entirely from the synoptic gradient, which is nearly flat: the authoritative-station mean is 3.7 kt. RYC is reporting S at 0.6 kt, 10-minute average 0.1 kt.

Direction coherence is extremely low (direction_agreement 0.19 across 26 stations). RYC reports S 188°, the western-LIS ensemble median is W 274°, KHPN inland is NW 320°, and the eastern Sound (KGON, KBID, KWST) shows SSE–S at 5–7 kt. These reflect a completely fragmented wind field — no coherent synoptic gradient is pushing through the cove. HRRR surface-wind direction changes 90–180° between each forecast hour (NNW → ENE → S → WNW → SE), which is the model generating near-random results in a near-calm environment, not a real wind signal.

The sea-breeze direction correction block shows a 61° model-obs divergence (HRRR at NNW 335°, western-LIS obs at W 274°), but the maturation correction is not applicable because the regime-A proxy is not met — deltaT and coastal_pct are both below their gates. Both the raw HRRR direction and the observed ensemble direction are unreliable in this calm setting.

Sunset occurs around 20:22 EDT. Because today's wind was never built on thermal forcing (B-light, not thermal A/A+), there is no sunset-collapse dynamic to manage — the wind will simply continue light and variable through the post-sunset hours.

Favored side: center. In light, variable conditions there is no systematic pressure gradient within the cove to exploit. Occasional NNW/W puffs off the land might give a momentary right-side lift on a typical N–S course, but the puffs are too short-lived and incoherent to commit to one side. Playing the center minimizes losses from any persistent random shift.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 observation is 91 min old — over-water wind data may be stale (last reading ~19:00 EDT)
